setting.note: In the summer of 1947, the small Punjab border village of Mano Majra, where Sikhs and Muslims have always lived as neighbours, is torn apart by Partition when a ghost train of massacred Sikhs arrives from the new Pakistan. Singh's spare, unsparing novel is rooted in the real geography of the frontier: the mixed village by its railway station, the bridge over the Sutlej, the river swollen with the monsoon and the dead, and the trains that carry a people to safety or to slaughter.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Khushwant Singh, first published 1956. Mapped as a cited work: no text stored, no passages reproduced. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: Train to Pakistan, Khushwant Singh (1956). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author's estate or publisher.

palette.note: The blood-red of the Partition massacres that reach even this quiet village, the horror the trains carry back and forth across the new border. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
